description_long: This is a traditional stone water well with a circular stone base constructed from roughly hewn, gray stones stacked in a ring. The well is topped with a wooden gabled roof made of overlapping, weathered wooden shingles, supported by two sturdy wooden posts. A horizontal wooden beam spans the posts, holding a hand-crank mechanism attached to a rope, which would typically be used to lower and raise a bucket. The crank handle is visibly mounted on one side, and the rope is coiled around a central spindle. The structure appears robust and utilitarian, with no visible decorative elements, and is designed to provide access to groundwater while offering some protection from debris. The overall appearance suggests durability and functionality, suitable for outdoor urban or park settings.
description: A circular stone water well with a wooden shingled roof and a hand-crank rope mechanism, supported by two wooden posts. The design is rustic and functional, with visible weathering on the wood and stone.
description_view_0: Front view showing the stone well, wooden posts, shingled roof, and the hand-crank mechanism clearly visible on the right.
description_view_1: Side view highlighting the triangular profile of the roof and the thickness of the wooden posts, with the stone well partially visible.
description_view_2: Rear view similar to the front, but the hand-crank mechanism is less visible, and the rope spindle is seen from behind.
description_view_3: Opposite side view showing the other end of the roof and posts, with the stone well base visible and no crank handle on this side.
attribute_presence_of_pulley_or_bucket_m: A hand-crank rope mechanism is present, mounted horizontally between the posts, with a visible crank handle and coiled rope.
attribute_surrounding_protective_structu: A circular stone wall surrounds the well opening, approximately waist-high, providing a protective barrier.
